title: [formatter] Add `magic-trailing-comma` option with `normalize` mode to make formatter COM-safe
body:
## Summary

Add a new [`magic-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#format_magic-trailing-comma) formatter option (`"respect"` | `"ignore"` | `"normalize"`) to replace the deprecated [`skip-magic-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#format_skip-magic-trailing-comma) boolean.

### Problem

The formatter and [`COM812`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/missing-trailing-comma/) (missing trailing comma) conflict. The formatter can produce compact multi-line output without a trailing comma:

```python
def args(
    aaaaaaaa, bbbbbbbbb, cccccccccc, ddddddddd, eeeeeeee, ffffff, gggggggggggg, hhhh
):
    pass
```

[`COM812`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/missing-trailing-comma/) flags this (newline before `)` without trailing comma), adds a comma, and the formatter then re-expands to one-per-line — requiring multiple passes to converge. This is impossible to automate in a single [`pre-commit`](https://pre-commit.com/) pass.

AI coding agents make this worse — they frequently produce over-wrapped expressions with unnecessary trailing commas that the formatter can't collapse:

```python
# AI-generated — stays expanded because trailing comma is "magic"
result = some_function(
    arg1,
    arg2,
)
```

The only workaround was disabling [`COM812`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/missing-trailing-comma/) entirely.

### Solution

The new `"normalize"` mode resolves this by making the formatter produce output that [`COM812`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/missing-trailing-comma/) has zero violations on in a single pass:

- Ignores existing trailing commas as expansion signals (like `"ignore"`)
- Eliminates the compact multi-line layout for function calls, definitions, pattern arguments, and subscript tuples — every multi-line expression is one-per-line with a trailing comma
- Removes unnecessary trailing commas from single-line expressions

```toml
[tool.ruff.format]
magic-trailing-comma = "normalize"

[tool.ruff.lint]
extend-select = ["COM812"]
```

### How it works

The formatter uses a two-group nesting structure for function call arguments and parameters. The inner `group()` enables a "compact multi-line" layout where all args pack on one indented line without a trailing comma. In `"normalize"` mode, this inner group is skipped — expressions go directly from single-line to one-per-line, matching the behavior already used by lists, dicts, sets, and imports.

Additionally, `JoinCommaSeparatedBuilder` defaults to `TrailingComma::OneOrMore` in Normalize mode, ensuring even single-entry sequences forced multi-line by comments get trailing commas.

### Examples

Over-wrapped AI-generated code collapses to one line:

```python
# Input
result = some_function(
    arg1,
    arg2,
)

# normalize output
result = some_function(arg1, arg2)
```

Genuinely long expressions get one-per-line with trailing comma (no compact multi-line):

```python
# Input (compact multi-line — COM812 would flag this)
result = some_function(
    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
)

# normalize output (one-per-line, COM812-safe)
result = some_function(
    first_argument,
    second_argument,
    third_argument,
    fourth_argument,
)
```

Single-line trailing commas removed:

```python
# Input
result = some_function(arg1, arg2,)
x = [1, 2, 3,]
def foo(a, b,): pass

# normalize output
result = some_function(arg1, arg2)
x = [1, 2, 3]
def foo(a, b): pass
```

Single-entry calls with comments get trailing commas:

```python
# Input
result = func(
    arg  # only arg
)

# normalize output
result = func(
    arg,  # only arg
)
```

Unparenthesized generators are protected (trailing comma would be `SyntaxError`):

```python
# Input — stays unchanged, no trailing comma added
result = sum(
    very_long_expression
    for very_long_expression in some_very_long_iterable_name
)
```

### Edge cases

- **Unparenthesized generators**: `sum(x for x in y)` — no trailing comma (would be `SyntaxError`). Parenthesized generators like `sum((x for x in y))` correctly get trailing commas. Distinguished via `ExprGenerator.parenthesized` AST field.
- **Single-element tuples**: `(1,)` — semantic comma preserved.
- **Argument hugging**: Disabled in Normalize mode — hugging skips the outer group, which would prevent `if_group_breaks` from adding trailing commas.
- **Subscript tuples**: `matrix[a, b, c]` — the `TupleParentheses::Preserve` path also skips the inner group in Normalize mode.
- **isort compatibility**: [`split-on-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#lint_isort_split-on-trailing-comma) is compatible with Normalize (both converge on the same output). [`force-wrap-aliases`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#lint_isort_force-wrap-aliases) still warns (genuine conflict — isort forces wrapping, formatter collapses).

### Config migration

| Old | New |
|-----|-----|
| [`skip-magic-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#format_skip-magic-trailing-comma) `= false` | [`magic-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#format_magic-trailing-comma) `= "respect"` |
| [`skip-magic-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#format_skip-magic-trailing-comma) `= true` | [`magic-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#format_magic-trailing-comma) `= "ignore"` |
| *(new)* | [`magic-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#format_magic-trailing-comma) `= "normalize"` |

Specifying both options errors with a clear message. The old boolean emits a deprecation warning.

### Warning changes

- [`COM812`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/missing-trailing-comma/) incompatibility warning now suggests `magic-trailing-comma = "normalize"` as the first recommendation
- Warning suppressed only when `"normalize"` is active (the only mode where the conflict is actually resolved)
- [`isort.split-on-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#lint_isort_split-on-trailing-comma) warning no longer fires for `"normalize"` (compatible)

### See also

- #9216
- https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/9216#issuecomment-4229572901

## Test Plan

- Snapshot tests with 55+ Python cases across all three modes covering: function calls, definitions, imports (including long module paths and `as` aliases), collection literals, subscripts, generators (parenthesized and unparenthesized), trailing/interleaved/above/below comments, noqa directives, single-line trailing comma removal, and compact multi-line elimination
- CLI integration tests for config acceptance, backward compatibility, dual-key conflict detection, and warning behavior ([`COM812`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/missing-trailing-comma/) suppression for normalize, [`isort.split-on-trailing-comma`](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#lint_isort_split-on-trailing-comma) compatibility)
- All 375 existing formatter tests pass with updated snapshots
author: XuehaiPan

linked_issues.title: How to use COM812 in linter but avoid conflict with formatter?

issue_comments.body:
The following:

```
# Input
result = func(
    arg
)
```

Should normalise to:

```
# normalize output
result = func(arg)
```

And the following:

```
# Input
result = func(
    arg,
)
```

Should also normalise to:

```
# normalize output
result = func(arg)
```

If arguments fit in a single line, then they shouldn't be expanded to one-per line. Honestly, I also find the follow utterly unreadable:

```
result = func(
   this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters
)
```

This last, at a glance, looks like it takes a single parameter, but won't get expanded unless we put a "magic" comma. The formatter should create clear and consistent output without needing to sprinkle magic for it.

Hi @WhyNotHugo, thanks for the feedback! All three cases are already handled by `normalize` mode in this PR (default line width = 88):

```python
# Input 1
result = func(
    arg
)
# normalize
result = func(arg)

# Input 2
result = func(
    arg,
)
# normalize
result = func(arg)

# Input 3 (fits within 88 chars)
result = func(
    this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters
)
# normalize
result = func(this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters)
```

When expressions fit on a single line, `normalize` collapses them and removes any trailing comma — regardless of the input formatting. No magic commas needed.

When they don't fit (or a comment forces multi-line), `normalize` expands to one-per-line with a trailing comma:

```python
# Input 3 with comment — can't collapse
result = func(
    # important call
    this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters
)
# normalize
result = func(
    # important call
    this_function_actually(takes, two),
    parameters,
)
```

I added test cases for these in the latest commit. Let me know if you see any edge cases I missed! Thanks!

I'll take a closer look on Monday, but I don't think the behavior of whether the compact arguments layout should be used should be coupled with `skip-magic-trailing-comma`. These are different concerns. 

The implementation in this PR also differs from what was discussed in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/9216 and it won't fix the COM812 incompatibility out of the box because the new "compact" mode is unlikely to become the default. This PR, therefore, does not resolve the COM812 incompatibility. Instead, it proposes a new formatter layout that can be configured.

> I don't think the behavior of whether the compact arguments layout should be used should be coupled with `skip-magic-trailing-comma`. These are different concerns.

That's a fair point. The one-per-line enforcement was added to ensure `COM812` convergence — without it, the formatter can produce `f(\n    a, b, c\n)` (multiple args on one indented line) which `COM812` flags. But I agree these are separable concerns. The layout could be controlled independently.

> The implementation in this PR also differs from what was discussed in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/9216 and it won't fix the COM812 incompatibility out of the box because the new "compact" mode is unlikely to become the default. This PR, therefore, does not resolve the COM812 incompatibility. Instead, it proposes a new formatter layout that can be configured.

Yes, the implementation in this PR differs from the `"force"` mode proposed in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/9216#issuecomment-1874384099. A `"force"` or `"add"` mode that always adds trailing commas to multi-line expressions would be compatible with `COM812` without directly changing the layout — though the added trailing comma would then cause the formatter to expand to one-per-line as a consequence.

The `"normalize"` mode proposed here is basically "add or remove based on whether the output fits within line length". This PR is resolving my comment https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/9216#issuecomment-4229572901 rather than the original issue in #9216. I can submit a separate PR for a `"force"`/`"add"` mode to resolve issue #9216.

Both `"force"` and `"normalize"` would be COM-safe and both result in one-per-line for multi-line expressions — the difference is that `"normalize"` additionally collapses over-wrapped code that fits on a single line. The motivation for both modes is the same: achieving style convergence in a single `ruff format` pass, without requiring multi-phase `format → check --fix → format` runs or `pre-commit` workarounds.

---

To illustrate the difference between the three modes with two inputs:

```python
# Input A: over-wrapped short call
result = func(
    arg1,
    arg2,
)

# Input B: genuinely long call
result = some_function(
    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
)
```

`"respect"` (default) — trailing comma is a signal to keep expanded:

```python
# A: stays expanded (trailing comma respected)
result = func(
    arg1,
    arg2,
)

# B: stays as-is (no trailing comma, no signal)
result = some_function(
    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
)
# COM812 flags B → adds comma → formatter re-expands → oscillation
```

`"force"` / `"add"` — always add trailing comma to multi-line:

```python
# A: stays expanded, trailing comma kept
result = func(
    arg1,
    arg2,
)

# B: trailing comma added → formatter expands to one-per-line
result = some_function(
    first_argument,
    second_argument,
    third_argument,
    fourth_argument,
)
# COM-safe ✓
```

`"normalize"` — add or remove based on line length:

```python
# A: collapsed (fits on one line, trailing comma removed)
result = func(arg1, arg2)

# B: one-per-line with trailing comma (doesn't fit on one line)
result = some_function(
    first_argument,
    second_argument,
    third_argument,
    fourth_argument,
)
# COM-safe ✓
```

If I understand the feature correctly, setting `skip-magic-trailing-comma = false` with a new option that disables the "all arguments on one line" layout should be enough to implement `normalize`. However, two options offer greater flexibility: users can disable the "all arguments on one line" layout while keeping the trailing comma behavior. 


However, I don't think we should introduce such an option. Instead, we should consider removing the "all arguments on one line" layout altogether if we consider it superior. @dylwil3 explored this in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/pull/23083. I don't remember what the conclusion of this PR was. 

If the conclusion was that this change is too disruptive, we should then consider introducing a `edition = "next"` that has the same stability guarantees as Ruff's stable formatter style, but which is allowed to deviate from black and allows us to make some of the more drastic changes (another one is changing how binary expressions are formatted), without forcing every project to reformat their code right away. But this requires some more consideration on how we want to roll this out. I don't think we have the capacity to explore it right now. Maybe once human-readable names, warning severity, and rule categorization are done later this year. 

For this PR, i don't think we should introduce a new option.

Honestly, I don't think this input should stay the same. It's neither being made to fit one line, nor expanded with one-line-per arg. It's a weird mix which is always confusing at a glance:

```
result = some_function(
    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
)
```

Considering the flag value is `normalise`, having an exception for it isn't ideal, we should just use the regular syntax for this?

```
result = some_function(
    first_argument,
    second_argument,
    third_argument,
    fourth_argument,
)
```

If normalise would remove the comma and wrap all arguments into a second line in the above, there's no way to make this code look sane again.

I'm not sure if I understand your comment but what I'm suggesting is that the formatter would no longer use:

```py
result = some_function(
    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
)
```

> I'm not sure if I understand your comment but what I'm suggesting is that the formatter would no longer use:
> 
> ```python
> result = some_function(
>     first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
> )
> ```

Yes, exactly. This example is a partially-reflowed one where the function call is neither in a single line nor one-arg-per line. AFAIK, this behaviour was mostly for backwards compatibility with `black`

Replies for review https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/pull/24572#issuecomment-4234566384 by @MichaReiser

> For this PR, I don't think we should introduce a new option.

Converted this PR to draft — the `"normalize"` mode is trickier than the simpler `"force"` approach discussed in https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/9216#issuecomment-1874384099. I've opened a new PR for the `"force"` feature under the `preview` guard in #24609.

> If I understand the feature correctly, setting `skip-magic-trailing-comma = false` with a new option that disables the "all arguments on one line" layout should be enough to implement `normalize`.

Not quite — `"normalize"` is more than just disabling the compact layout. Once `"force"` lands, `"normalize"` is essentially a single-pass equivalent of `"ignore"` followed by `"force"`: first ignore existing trailing commas as expansion signals (collapsing over-wrapped expressions that fit on one line), then add trailing commas to all hanging multi-line expressions. The `"normalize"` option exists because this two-step behavior can't be achieved with a single `ruff format` pass using either `"ignore"` or `"force"` alone.

The original issue — compact multi-line without trailing comma (`COM812` conflict):

```python
# Input
result = some_function(
    first_argument_name, second_argument_name, third_argument_name, fourth_argument_name
)

# respect — stays compact, COM812 flags it → oscillation
result = some_function(
    first_argument_name, second_argument_name, third_argument_name, fourth_argument_name
)

# ignore — stays compact, COM812 flags it → oscillation
result = some_function(
    first_argument_name, second_argument_name, third_argument_name, fourth_argument_name
)

# force — trailing comma added → one-per-line, COM812 safe ✓
result = some_function(
    first_argument_name,
    second_argument_name,
    third_argument_name,
    fourth_argument_name,
)

# normalize — same result as force for this case ✓
result = some_function(
    first_argument_name,
    second_argument_name,
    third_argument_name,
    fourth_argument_name,
)
```

Over-wrapped short expression with trailing comma (AI-generated code):

```python
# Input
result = func(
    arg1,
    arg2,
)

# respect — stays expanded (trailing comma is "magic")
result = func(
    arg1,
    arg2,
)

# ignore — collapses, but COM812 unsafe on long expressions
result = func(arg1, arg2)

# force — stays expanded (trailing comma is still "magic")
result = func(
    arg1,
    arg2,
)

# normalize — collapses AND is COM812 safe
result = func(arg1, arg2)
```

`"force"` resolves the original `COM812` conflict (#9216). `"normalize"` additionally collapses over-wrapped AI-generated code — a follow-up concern from https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/9216#issuecomment-4229572901.

I'm still not a 100% sure I understand the difference to `ignore` and just removing the 

```
  result = some_function(
      first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
  )
```

layout. But I'm still convinced we shouldn't introduce a new option, and that it shouldn't be coupled with the magic trailing-comma handling. Instead, this new layout should become the default if we're convinced that the all arguments on one line layout is less readable. I'll wait for @dylwil3's input but I think we should convert this into an issue where we discuss the style change before putting up multiple PRs.

I think the next step here is to open an issue with a concise summary of the preferred formatting, decoupled from whether this should be behind an option. This allows us to make a decision on 

a) Is this a formatting we want to support
b) Should this become the default and, if so, how do we get there
c) If it shouldn't become the default, how to make it configurable?

> I think the next step here is to open an issue

Should we open a new issue or discuss under the existing one?

- #9216
I suggest opening a new issue and mention that this style change also addresses #9216 if it becomes the new default style.

@@ -0,0 +1,419 @@
+###
+# Function calls
+###
+
+# Over-wrapped with trailing comma
+result = some_function(
+    arg1,
+    arg2,
+)
+
+# Over-wrapped without trailing comma
+result = some_function(
+    arg1,
+    arg2
+)
+
+# Already on one line
+result = some_function(arg1, arg2)
+
+# Single-line trailing comma
+result = some_function(arg1, arg2,)
+
+# Genuinely long
+result = some_function(
+    first_argument,
+    second_argument,
+    third_argument,
+    fourth_argument,
+    fifth_argument,
+)
+
+# Compact multi-line without trailing comma
+result = some_function(
+    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
+)
+
+# Keyword args, over-wrapped
+result = func(
+    key1=value1,
+    key2=value2,
+)
+
+# Single arg with trailing comment
+result = func(
+    a,  # only arg
+)
+
+# Trailing comment without comma
+result = func(
+    arg1,
+    arg2  # no comma here
+)
+
+# Trailing comment without comma, single arg
+result = func(
+    arg1  # no comma here either
+)
+
+# Trailing comment on last arg
+result = func(
+    arg1,
+    arg2,  # important arg
+)
+
+# Trailing comments on every arg
+result = func(
+    arg1,  # first
+    arg2,  # second
+)
+
+# Interleaved comments
+result = func(
+    # group 1
+    arg1,
+    arg2,
+    # group 2
+    arg3,
+    arg4,
+)
+
+# Comment after opening paren
+result = func(
+    # this is a config call
+    arg1,
+    arg2,
+)
+
+# Comment before closing paren
+result = func(
+    arg1,
+    arg2,
+    # end of args
+)
+
+# Short args with trailing comment
+result = func(
+    a,  # keep this
+    b,
+)
+
+# noqa directives (formatter ignores them)
+result = func(
+    arg1,
+    arg2  # noqa: COM812
+)
+result = func(
+    arg1,
+    arg2,  # noqa: COM819
+)
+result = func(
+    arg1,
+    arg2,
+)  # noqa: COM812
+
+# Compact multi-line with comment above
+result = some_function(
+    # these are the important arguments
+    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
+)
+
+# Compact multi-line with comment below
+result = some_function(
+    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument
+    # end of arguments
+)
+
+# Compact multi-line with trailing comment
+result = some_function(
+    first_argument, second_argument, third_argument, fourth_argument  # all on one line
+)
+
+# Nested calls
+result = outer(
+    inner(
+        a,
+        b,
+    ),
+    c,
+)
+
+# Deeply nested
+result = outer(
+    inner(
+        deep(
+            x,
+            y,
+        ),
+    ),
+)
+
+
+###
+# Function definitions
+###
+
+# Over-wrapped with trailing comma
+def foo(
+    a,
+    b,
+):
+    pass
+
+
+# Single-line trailing comma
+def baz(a, b,): pass
+
+# Compact multi-line
+def bar(
+    first_param, second_param, third_param, fourth_param, fifth_param
+):
+    pass
+
+
+# Compact multi-line with comment above
+def baz2(
+    # positional params
+    first_param, second_param, third_param, fourth_param, fifth_param
+):
+    pass
+
+
+# Compact multi-line with comment below
+def qux(
+    first_param, second_param, third_param, fourth_param, fifth_param
+    # end of params
+):
+    pass
+
+
+# Interleaved comments
+def bar2(
+    # positional args
+    a,
+    b,
+    # keyword-only args
+    *,
+    c,
+    d,
+):
+    pass
+
+
+###
+# Collection literals
+###
+
+# List, over-wrapped
+items = [
+    1,
+    2,
+]
+
+# List, single-line trailing comma
+x = [1, 2, 3,]
+
+# Dict, over-wrapped
+config = {
+    "key1": "value1",
+    "key2": "value2",
+}
+
+# Dict, single-line trailing comma
+config = {"key": "value",}
+
+# Set, over-wrapped
+unique = {
+    1,
+    2,
+}
+
+# Single-element tuple (semantic comma)
+x = (1,)
+
+# Single-element tuple, over-wrapped
+x = (
+    1,
+)
+
+# List with interleaved comments
+items = [
+    # first group
+    1,
+    2,
+    # second group
+    3,
+    4,
+]
+
+# Dict with trailing comments
+config = {
+    "key1": "value1",  # primary
+    "key2": "value2",  # secondary
+}
+
+
+###
+# Imports
+###
+
+# Over-wrapped with trailing comma
+from module import (
+    name1,
+    name2,
+)
+
+# Single name, over-wrapped
+from module import (
+    name1,
+)
+
+# Single name, no trailing comma
+from module import (
+    name1
+)
+
+# Genuinely long
+from very.long.module.path.that.goes.on import (
+    first_name,
+    second_name,
+    third_name,
+    fourth_name,
+)
+
+# Genuinely long, missing trailing comma
+from very.long.module.path.that.goes.on import (
+    first_name,
+    second_name,
+    third_name,
+    fourth_name
+)
+
+# Compact multi-line
+from very.long.module.path.that.goes.on import (
+    first_name, second_name, third_name, fourth_name
+)
+
+# Single line with trailing comma
+from module import (name1, name2,)
+
+# Star import
+from module import *
+
+# With aliases
+from module import (
+    name1 as alias1,
+    name2 as alias2,
+)
+
+# With comment
+from module import (
+    name1,
+    name2,  # keep this
+)
+
+# Very long module path, single name (fits on one line)
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import long_long_long_name
+
+# Very long module path, single name (exceeds line length)
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import some_extremely_long_name_for_function
+
+# Very long module path, multiple names (exceeds line length without parens)
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import first_long_name, second_long_name_x
+
+# Very long module path, multiple names (already parenthesized)
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import (
+    first_long_name,
+    second_long_name,
+)
+
+# Very long module path, short name with parens
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import (
+    short,
+)
+
+# Plain import with alias
+import very.long.module.path.name as short_name
+
+
+###
+# Subscripts
+###
+
+# Compact multi-line
+result = matrix[
+    first_long_index, second_long_index, third_long_index, fourth_long_index
+]
+
+# Over-wrapped with trailing comma
+result = matrix[
+    first_index,
+    second_index,
+    third_index,
+]
+
+# Short subscript
+result = matrix[a, b]
+
+# Single-line trailing comma
+result = matrix[a, b,]
+
+# Genuinely long
+result = very_long_matrix_name[
+    first_very_long_index_name,
+    second_very_long_index_name,
+    third_very_long_index_name,
+]
+
+
+###
+# Generators
+###
+
+# Unparenthesized sole generator — trailing comma would be SyntaxError
+result = sum(x for x in range(10))
+
+# Unparenthesized generator that wraps
+result = sum(
+    very_long_expression for very_long_expression in some_very_long_iterable_name
+)
+
+# Parenthesized generator — trailing comma is valid
+result = sum(
+    (x for x in range(10)),
+)
+
+# Parenthesized generator that wraps
+result = sum(
+    (very_long_expression for very_long_expression in some_very_long_iterable_name)
+)
+
+# Nested call as argument, compact multi-line
+result = func(
+    this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters
+)
+
+# Nested call as argument, with comment above
+result = func(
+    # important call
+    this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters
+)
+
+# Nested call as argument, with trailing comment
+result = func(
+    this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters  # both args
+)
+
+# Nested call as argument, with comment below
+result = func(
+    this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters
+    # end of args
+)
+
+# Mixed: some args fit, some don't
+result = func(short, another_short, a_third_short_one)
+result = func(
+    very_long_argument_name_one,
+    very_long_argument_name_two,
+    very_long_argument_name_three,
+)

+x = [1, 2, 3]
+
+# Dict, over-wrapped
+config = {"key1": "value1", "key2": "value2"}
+
+# Dict, single-line trailing comma
+config = {"key": "value"}
+
+# Set, over-wrapped
+unique = {1, 2}
+
+# Single-element tuple (semantic comma)
+x = (1,)
+
+# Single-element tuple, over-wrapped
+x = (1,)
+
+# List with interleaved comments
+items = [
+    # first group
+    1,
+    2,
+    # second group
+    3,
+    4,
+]
+
+# Dict with trailing comments
+config = {
+    "key1": "value1",  # primary
+    "key2": "value2",  # secondary
+}
+
+
+###
+# Imports
+###
+
+# Over-wrapped with trailing comma
+from module import name1, name2
+
+# Single name, over-wrapped
+from module import name1
+
+# Single name, no trailing comma
+from module import name1
+
+# Genuinely long
+from very.long.module.path.that.goes.on import (
+    first_name,
+    second_name,
+    third_name,
+    fourth_name,
+)
+
+# Genuinely long, missing trailing comma
+from very.long.module.path.that.goes.on import (
+    first_name,
+    second_name,
+    third_name,
+    fourth_name,
+)
+
+# Compact multi-line
+from very.long.module.path.that.goes.on import (
+    first_name,
+    second_name,
+    third_name,
+    fourth_name,
+)
+
+# Single line with trailing comma
+from module import name1, name2
+
+# Star import
+from module import *
+
+# With aliases
+from module import name1 as alias1, name2 as alias2
+
+# With comment
+from module import (
+    name1,
+    name2,  # keep this
+)
+
+# Very long module path, single name (fits on one line)
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import long_long_long_name
+
+# Very long module path, single name (exceeds line length)
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import (
+    some_extremely_long_name_for_function,
+)
+
+# Very long module path, multiple names (exceeds line length without parens)
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import (
+    first_long_name,
+    second_long_name_x,
+)
+
+# Very long module path, multiple names (already parenthesized)
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import first_long_name, second_long_name
+
+# Very long module path, short name with parens
+from very_long_module.deeply_nested.sub_package import short
+
+# Plain import with alias
+import very.long.module.path.name as short_name
+
+
+###
+# Subscripts
+###
+
+# Compact multi-line
+result = matrix[
+    first_long_index,
+    second_long_index,
+    third_long_index,
+    fourth_long_index,
+]
+
+# Over-wrapped with trailing comma
+result = matrix[first_index, second_index, third_index]
+
+# Short subscript
+result = matrix[a, b]
+
+# Single-line trailing comma
+result = matrix[a, b]
+
+# Genuinely long
+result = very_long_matrix_name[
+    first_very_long_index_name,
+    second_very_long_index_name,
+    third_very_long_index_name,
+]
+
+
+###
+# Generators
+###
+
+# Unparenthesized sole generator — trailing comma would be SyntaxError
+result = sum(x for x in range(10))
+
+# Unparenthesized generator that wraps
+result = sum(
+    very_long_expression
+    for very_long_expression in some_very_long_iterable_name
+)
+
+# Parenthesized generator — trailing comma is valid
+result = sum((x for x in range(10)))
+
+# Parenthesized generator that wraps
+result = sum(
+    (very_long_expression for very_long_expression in some_very_long_iterable_name),
+)
+
+# Nested call as argument, compact multi-line
+result = func(this_function_actually(takes, two), parameters)
+
+# Nested call as argument, with comment above
+result = func(
+    # important call
+    this_function_actually(takes, two),
+    parameters,
+)
+
+# Nested call as argument, with trailing comment
+result = func(
+    this_function_actually(takes, two),
+    parameters,  # both args
+)
+
+# Nested call as argument, with comment below
+result = func(
+    this_function_actually(takes, two),
+    parameters,
+    # end of args
+)
+
+# Mixed: some args fit, some don't
+result = func(short, another_short, a_third_short_one)
+result = func(
+    very_long_argument_name_one,
+    very_long_argument_name_two,
+    very_long_argument_name_three,
+)
+```
